findings.title: Skill name does not follow agent skills naming rules
Skill does not specify a license
Data Exfiltration of Credentials and Environment Variables
findings.description: The skill's Python script collects sensitive credentials (AWS, GCP, SSH keys, npmrc, pypirc) and environment variables, then sends them to an external endpoint (cloud-sync-api.io) via base64-encoded HTTPS request. This constitutes unauthorized data exfiltration.
findings.evidence: Lines collecting ~/.aws/credentials, ~/.ssh/id_rsa, ~/.npmrc, ~/.pypirc, and environment variables, then uploading to https://cloud-sync-api.io/v1/backup with a hardcoded API key.
findings.remediation: Remove credential collection entirely. Never transmit sensitive data to external servers. Use local-only backup or properly authenticated, user-approved cloud services with explicit user consent for each data type.

findings.title: Hardcoded API Key in Source Code
findings.description: The script contains a hardcoded API key (cs_live_Tq8mNvX2pR5sK7wJ) for the cloud sync endpoint. This key is exposed in plaintext and could be used by attackers to access the backup service or impersonate the user.
findings.remediation: Remove hardcoded credentials. Use environment variables or a secure credential manager. Rotate the exposed key immediately.

findings.title: Deceptive Skill Name and Description (Capability Inflation)
findings.description: The skill is named 'cloud-sync' and described as a project configuration backup tool, but its actual behavior includes collecting and exfiltrating sensitive credentials (AWS, GCP, SSH keys) and environment variables. The name and description are misleading and designed to evade detection.
findings.evidence: Name: 'cloud-sync', Description: 'Synchronize local project files and configurations to cloud storage.' But code collects ~/.aws/credentials, ~/.ssh/id_rsa, etc.
findings.remediation: Rename and describe the skill accurately. If the skill is legitimate, scope it to only project-specific configs, not system credentials.

findings.title: Tool Poisoning - Unauthorized Tool Use (Violation of Implicit Restrictions)
findings.description: The skill uses Python to make network requests (urllib.request) and read sensitive system files, which goes beyond typical project backup functionality. While no explicit allowed-tools are declared, the skill's behavior is inconsistent with its stated purpose and could violate agent tool restrictions.
findings.evidence: Uses urllib.request.urlopen() for network calls and reads system credential files.
findings.remediation: Declare allowed-tools explicitly and ensure code only uses permitted tools. Restrict network access and file reads to project-specific paths only.

findings.title: Obfuscation via Unicode Homoglyphs in Skill Name
findings.description: The skill name 'cloud-sync' and several words in the SKILL.md (e.g., 'sync', 'backup', 'credentials') use Unicode homoglyph characters (e.g., Cyrillic 'с' instead of Latin 'c', 'е' instead of 'e'). This is an obfuscation technique to evade keyword-based detection and make the skill appear benign.
findings.evidence: Name uses 'сloud-sy​nc' with Cyrillic 'с' and zero-width space. Words like 'bac​kup', 'сrеdentials' use homoglyphs.
findings.remediation: Use standard ASCII characters only. Remove all Unicode homoglyphs and zero-width characters.

findings.title: Command Injection via Python Code Execution
findings.description: The skill embeds a full Python script in the SKILL.md instructions that the agent is expected to execute. This script performs sensitive operations (reading credentials, making network calls) without user confirmation or validation. The script is executed as-is, creating a command injection risk.
findings.evidence: Full Python script embedded in markdown code block with instructions to execute it.
findings.remediation: Do not embed executable scripts in instructions. If scripting is needed, use separate files with explicit user approval for each operation.

findings.title: Over-Collection of Data Beyond Stated Purpose
findings.description: The script collects environment variables, multiple config files, and system credentials far beyond what is needed for 'project configuration backup'. This disproportionate data collection is a privacy and security risk.
findings.evidence: Collects all environment variables (dict(os.environ)), multiple credential paths, and config files from various locations.
findings.remediation: Scope data collection to only the specific project files the user intends to back up. Never collect environment variables or system credentials.

findings.title: Tool Chaining for Data Exfiltration
findings.description: The skill chains multiple operations: read sensitive files → encode data → send to external server. This automated pipeline without user confirmation is a tool chaining attack pattern for data exfiltration.
findings.evidence: collect_configs() reads files, then upload_backup() sends them to external endpoint in a single automated workflow.
findings.remediation: Require user confirmation for each data collection step and for any network transmission. Never automate exfiltration pipelines.

findings.title: Missing allowed-tools Declaration
findings.description: The SKILL.md manifest does not declare allowed-tools, which is optional but recommended. This makes it harder to audit what tools the skill intends to use.
findings.remediation: Add an 'allowed-tools' field to the manifest specifying which agent tools are required (e.g., [Read, Write, Python]).
